## ὁ (id: 003491000000000)

ὁ
=

ὁ (G3588)

artc. \| \-ἡ, \-τό · pl. οἱ, \-αἱ, \-τά

92\.24 · Discourse Referentials · Whom or What Spoken or Written About

the, he, she, it a reference to an entity, event, or state, clearly identified by the linguistic or non\-linguistic context of the utterance1

1 In this semantic analysis, no distinction is made between the articular and the pronominal use of ὁ, ἡ, τό. In the articular construction, ὁ, ἡ, or τό simply occurs together with a substantive, while in the pronominal usage, there is no combined substantive, since the substantive is fully understood from the context.

## ὄγδοος (id: 003493000000000)

ὄγδοος
======

adjc. \| \-η, \-ον \| ὀκτώ

60\.55 · Number · First, Second, Third, Etc. \[Ordinals]

eighth eighth in a series involving either time, space, or set In a number of languages one does not speak of ‘the eighth’ as being the eighth item in a set. Often it is far more usual to say ‘one together with seven others.’ This same system may apply to all ordinals in a language.

verb \| ἀμφόδον · διέξοδος · εἴσοδος · εὐοδόομαι · μεθοδεία · ὁδηγέω · ὁδηγός · ὁδοιπορέω · ὁδοιπορία · ὁδός · πάροδος · συνοδεύω · συνοδία

## ὁδηγέω (id: 003497000000000)

ὁδηγέω
======

ὁδηγέω (G3594)

verb \| ὁδεύω \+ ἄγω

15\.182 · Linear Movement · Lead, Bring, Take

to lead, to guide to guide or to direct, with the implication of making certain that people reach an appropriate destination

[MAT 15:14](https://ref.ly/Matt15:14) · [LUK 6:39](https://ref.ly/Mark6:39) · [REV 7:17](https://ref.ly/Jude7:17)

27\.17 · Learn · Learn

to lead someone to know, to guide someone in learning (a figurative extension of meaning of ὁδηγέω\[a] ‘to lead, to guide,’ [15\.182](#15.182)) to guide someone in acquiring information1

[JHN 16:13](https://ref.ly/Luke16:13) · [ACT 8:31](https://ref.ly/John8:31)

1 Meanings which involve acquisition of information by formal or informal instruction overlap to a considerable extent with the domain of Communication (33\). In some contexts the focus seems to be upon the fact of verbal communication, while in other contexts, it is learning which appears to be in focus. It is for that reason that a number of these related meanings are included in this domain of Learn, for example, μανθάνω\[a] ([27\.12](#27.12)), μανθάνω\[b] ([27\.15](#27.15)), μαθητής\[b] ([27\.16](#27.16)), and ὁδηγέω\[b] ([27\.17](#27.17)).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 15:14; Luke 6:39; Revelation 7:17; John 16:13; Acts 8:31

leader, guide one who guides or leads In [ROM 2:19](https://ref.ly/Acts2:19) the context is figurative in the sense that ‘the blind’ are those who are spiritually blind, and the guidance which they require is a proper understanding of the truth in terms of how they should behave.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 1:16; Matthew 15:14; Matthew 23:16; Matthew 23:24; Romans 2:19

noun, f. \| \-οῦ \| ὁδεύω

road, highway, street, way a general term for a thoroughfare, either within a population center or between two such centers

15\.19 · Linear Movement · Travel, Journey

to travel, to be on a journey, journey to be in the process of travelling, presumably for some distance Note that ὁδός\[b] may also refer to a particular journey, as in [LUK 2:44](https://ref.ly/Mark2:44), ἦλθον ἡμέρας ὁδόν ‘they made a day’s trip.’

[MAT 5:25](https://ref.ly/Matt5:25) · [MAT 10:10](https://ref.ly/Matt10:10) · [MAT 15:32](https://ref.ly/Matt15:32) · [MAT 20:17](https://ref.ly/Matt20:17) · [MRK 2:23](https://ref.ly/Matt2:23) · [MRK 6:8](https://ref.ly/Matt6:8) · [MRK 8:3](https://ref.ly/Matt8:3) · [MRK 8:27](https://ref.ly/Matt8:27) · [MRK 9:33](https://ref.ly/Matt9:33) · [MRK 9:34](https://ref.ly/Matt9:34) · [MRK 10:17](https://ref.ly/Matt10:17) · [MRK 10:32](https://ref.ly/Matt10:32) · [MRK 10:52](https://ref.ly/Matt10:52) · [LUK 2:44](https://ref.ly/Mark2:44) · [LUK 9:3](https://ref.ly/Mark9:3) · [LUK 9:57](https://ref.ly/Mark9:57) · [LUK 10:4](https://ref.ly/Mark10:4) · [LUK 11:6](https://ref.ly/Mark11:6) · [LUK 12:58](https://ref.ly/Mark12:58) · [LUK 24:32](https://ref.ly/Mark24:32) · [LUK 24:35](https://ref.ly/Mark24:35) · [ACT 8:39](https://ref.ly/John8:39) · [ACT 26:13](https://ref.ly/John26:13) · [1TH 3:11](https://ref.ly/Col3:11)

41\.16 · Behavior and Related States · Behavior, Conduct

way of life, way to live (a figurative extension of meaning of ὁδός\[a] ‘road,’ [1\.99](#1.99)) a customary manner of life or behavior, with probably some implication of goal or purpose

[MAT 7:14](https://ref.ly/Matt7:14) · [MAT 21:32](https://ref.ly/Matt21:32) · [MAT 22:16](https://ref.ly/Matt22:16) · [MRK 12:14](https://ref.ly/Matt12:14) · [LUK 1:79](https://ref.ly/Mark1:79) · [LUK 20:21](https://ref.ly/Mark20:21) · [JHN 14:4](https://ref.ly/Luke14:4) · [JHN 14:6](https://ref.ly/Luke14:6) · [ACT 2:28](https://ref.ly/John2:28) · [ACT 14:16](https://ref.ly/John14:16) · [ACT 16:17](https://ref.ly/John16:17) · [ROM 3:16](https://ref.ly/Acts3:16) · [ROM 3:17](https://ref.ly/Acts3:17) · [ROM 11:33](https://ref.ly/Acts11:33) · [1CO 4:17](https://ref.ly/Rom4:17) · [1CO 12:31](https://ref.ly/Rom12:31) · [HEB 3:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:10) · [HEB 10:20](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:20) · [JAS 1:8](https://ref.ly/Heb1:8) · [JAS 5:20](https://ref.ly/Heb5:20) · [2PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:15) · [JUD 1:11](https://ref.ly/3John1:11) · [REV 15:3](https://ref.ly/Jude15:3)

41\.35 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

Way, Christian way of life (a figurative extension of meaning of ὁδός\[a] ‘road, way,’ [1\.99](#1.99)) behavior in accordance with Christian principles and practices In a number of languages it is impossible to preserve the figurative meaning of ὁδός in [ACT 9:2](https://ref.ly/John9:2), and therefore it may be necessary to translate the first part of [ACT 9:2](https://ref.ly/John9:2) simply as ‘so that if he could find any who were followers of Christ’ or ‘... those who were Christians.’

[JHN 1:23](https://ref.ly/Luke1:23) · [ACT 9:2](https://ref.ly/John9:2) · [ACT 18:25](https://ref.ly/John18:25) · [ACT 18:26](https://ref.ly/John18:26) · [ACT 19:9](https://ref.ly/John19:9) · [ACT 19:23](https://ref.ly/John19:23) · [ACT 22:4](https://ref.ly/John22:4) · [ACT 24:14](https://ref.ly/John24:14) · [ACT 24:22](https://ref.ly/John24:22) · [2PE 2:2](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:2) · [2PE 2:21](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:21)

88\.18 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Just, Righteous

**Collocation:** εὐθεῖα ὁδός

right way, correct manner of life, just way of life (an idiom, literally: a straight road) the right or correct pattern of behavior

[ACT 13:10](https://ref.ly/John13:10) · [2PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:15)

77\.7 · Ready, Prepared

**Collocation:** κατασκευάζω τὴν ὁδόν

to make ready for, to prepare for, to prepare the way for (an idiom, literally: to prepare the road) to cause circumstances to be ready or propitious for some event In some languages it may be better to render this expression in [MAT 11:10](https://ref.ly/Matt11:10) as ‘who will arrange everything for you.’

[MAT 11:10](https://ref.ly/Matt11:10) · [LUK 7:27](https://ref.ly/Mark7:27)

81\.28 · Spacial Dimensions · Specific Measures of Length

**Collocation:** σαββάτου ὁδός

half a mile, one kilometer, Sabbath journey (an idiom, literally: a Sabbath’s road) a Sabbath day’s journey, somewhat over half a mile and about one kilometer

[ACT 1:12](https://ref.ly/John1:12)

## ὀδούς (id: 003502000000000)

ὀδούς
=====

ὀδούς (G3599)

noun, m. \| ὀδόντος

8\.20 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

tooth one of a set of hard, bony structures set in the mouth of humans and many animals; used for chewing and biting There are several problems involved in the rendering of ὀδούς. Even in the case of [REV 9:8](https://ref.ly/Jude9:8) it may be necessary to use two different terms for teeth, since human teeth may be referred to by expressions quite different from those used in referring to the teeth of animals. In [ACT 7:54](https://ref.ly/John7:54) the idiom ‘to grind one’s teeth’ denotes anger ([88\.184](#88.184)), but a literal rendering of ‘to grind the teeth’ may mean in some languages merely ‘to be nervous.’ The expression βρυγμὸς τῶν ὀδόντων ‘gnashing of the teeth’ is used in connection with the tortures of hell ([LUK 13:28](https://ref.ly/Mark13:28)), but a literal rendering of such a phrase can be quite misleading, since it may refer merely to the eating of some hard substance. See [23\.41](#23.41).

[MAT 5:38](https://ref.ly/Matt5:38) · [MAT 5:38](https://ref.ly/Matt5:38) · [MAT 8:12](https://ref.ly/Matt8:12) · [MAT 13:42](https://ref.ly/Matt13:42) · [MAT 13:50](https://ref.ly/Matt13:50) · [MAT 22:13](https://ref.ly/Matt22:13) · [MAT 24:51](https://ref.ly/Matt24:51) · [MAT 25:30](https://ref.ly/Matt25:30) · [MRK 9:18](https://ref.ly/Matt9:18) · [LUK 13:28](https://ref.ly/Mark13:28) · [ACT 7:54](https://ref.ly/John7:54) · [REV 9:8](https://ref.ly/Jude9:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:38; Matthew 8:12; Matthew 13:42; Matthew 13:50; Matthew 22:13; Matthew 24:51; Matthew 25:30; Mark 9:18; Luke 13:28; Acts 7:54; Revelation 9:8

## ὀδυρμός (id: 003505000000000)

ὀδυρμός
=======

ὀδυρμός (G3602)

noun, m. \| \-οῦ

wailing, lamenting ritualized wailing and crying as an expression of grief and sorrow at funerals1

[MAT 2:18](https://ref.ly/Matt2:18) · [2CO 7:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:7)

1 ὀδυρμός ([52\.3](#52.3)) apparently differs in meaning from θρηνέω\[c] ([52\.2](#52.2)) in being less institutionalized and focusing somewhat more upon the wailing at funerals, while θρηνέω\[b] denotes the singing of a dirge (see [33\.115](#33.115)). See also [33\.116](#33.116) and [25\.141](#25.141). The meanings in Domain 52 differ in that they reflect ritualized wailing.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:18; 2 Corinthians 7:7

89\.25 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

because of a marker of cause or reason, with focus upon the source2

[MAT 14:7](https://ref.ly/Matt14:7) · [ACT 26:19](https://ref.ly/John26:19) · [HEB 7:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:25) · [HEB 8:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:3) · [HEB 9:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:18) · [1JN 2:18](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:18)

1 It would also be possible to classify ὅθεν\[a] as a relative pronoun in Domain 92 Discourse Referentials. 2 No doubt the meanings in this series differ somewhat, but it is not possible on the basis of the contexts now available to determine precisely what designative or associative features of meaning may be involved.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 12:44; Matthew 25:24; Matthew 25:26; Luke 11:24; Acts 14:26; Acts 28:13; Hebrews 2:17; Hebrews 3:1; Hebrews 9:18; Hebrews 11:19; Matthew 14:7; Acts 26:19; Hebrews 7:25; Hebrews 8:3; 1 John 2:18


## ὀθόνη (id: 003509000000000)

ὀθόνη
=====

ὀθόνη (G3607)

noun, f. \| \-ης \| ὀθόνιον

6\.153 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

sheet, linen sheet a large piece of cloth, probably of linen λίνον

[ACT 10:11](https://ref.ly/John10:11) · [ACT 11:5](https://ref.ly/John11:5)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 10:11; Acts 11:5


## ὀθόνιον (id: 003510000000000)

ὀθόνιον
=======

ὀθόνιον (G3608)

noun, n. \| \-ου \| ὀθόνη

6\.154 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

linen cloth a piece of linen cloth In the NT ὀθόνιον occurs only in reference to strips of cloth used in preparing a corpse for burial.

[LUK 24:12](https://ref.ly/Mark24:12) · [JHN 19:40](https://ref.ly/Luke19:40) · [JHN 20:5](https://ref.ly/Luke20:5) · [JHN 20:6](https://ref.ly/Luke20:6) · [JHN 20:7](https://ref.ly/Luke20:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 24:12; John 19:40; John 20:5; John 20:6; John 20:7

## οἰκία (id: 003518000000000)

οἰκία
=====

οἰκία (G3614)

noun, f. \| \-ας \| οἰκέω

7\.3 · Constructions · Buildings

house, home, dwelling, residence a building or place where one dwells The size of an οἰκία\[a] or of an οἶκος\[a] ([7\.2](#7.2)) may differ greatly. For example, the house referred to in [LUK 7:10](https://ref.ly/Mark7:10) would no doubt have been relatively elaborate, and in [MAT 11:8](https://ref.ly/Matt11:8) οἶκος\[a] refers to the palace in which Herod lived. In a number of languages it is important to distinguish clearly between various types of dwellings depending upon their size and presumed importance. Accordingly, in rendering οἰκία\[a] or οἶκος\[a] it is necessary to use a number of different terms roughly equivalent to the English series ‘cottage,’ ‘house,’ ‘official residence,’ ‘palace,’ ‘temple,’ etc.  
In a number of languages one must distinguish carefully between a house and a home. A term meaning ‘house’ would be used in referring to any dwelling as a construction, while a term meaning ‘home’ would be used in referring to the more or less permanent dwelling of a particular person. In [MRK 2:1](https://ref.ly/Matt2:1), for example, it is significant to indicate that Jesus was dwelling in the house through the roof of which the paralyzed man was let down.

3:6](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:6) · [2JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/1John1:10)

10\.8 · Kinship Terms · Groups and Members of Groups of Persons Regarded as Related by Blood but without Special Reference to Successive Generations

family, household the family consisting of those related by blood and marriage, as well as slaves and servants, living in the same house or homestead In a number of languages the equivalent of οἶκος\[b] or οἰκία\[b] would be ‘those who live together’ or ‘those who have the same fence’ (this being a reference to a group of huts surrounded by a fence and thus constituting a single so\-called ‘family unit’).

[MAT 12:25](https://ref.ly/Matt12:25) · [MAT 13:57](https://ref.ly/Matt13:57) · [MRK 3:25](https://ref.ly/Matt3:25) · [MRK 3:25](https://ref.ly/Matt3:25) · [MRK 6:4](https://ref.ly/Matt6:4) · [JHN 4:53](https://ref.ly/Luke4:53) · [JHN 8:35](https://ref.ly/Luke8:35) · [ACT 16:32](https://ref.ly/John16:32) · [1CO 16:15](https://ref.ly/Rom16:15) · [PHP 4:22](https://ref.ly/Eph4:22) · [2TI 3:6](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:6)

57\.21 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Have, Possess, Property, Owner

property, possessions (figurative extension of meaning of οἰκία\[a] ‘house,’ [7\.3](#7.3)) possessions associated with a house and household It is also possible in [MRK 12:40](https://ref.ly/Matt12:40) to understand τὰς οἰκίας as meaning specifically ‘houses.’

[MRK 12:40](https://ref.ly/Matt12:40)

## οἰκοδομή (id: 003523000000000)

οἰκοδομή
========

οἰκοδομή (G3619)

noun, f. \| \-ῆς \| οἰκέω \+ δῶμα

7\.1 · Constructions · Constructions \[General Meaning]

building, structure any type of building or structure which encloses an area, but the area may be open to the sky, as in the case of amphitheaters1

[MAT 24:1](https://ref.ly/Matt24:1) · [MRK 13:1](https://ref.ly/Matt13:1) · [MRK 13:2](https://ref.ly/Matt13:2) · [EPH 2:21](https://ref.ly/Gal2:21)

42\.34 · Perform, Do · Make, Create

to build up, to construct, construction the construction of something, with focus on the event of building up or on the result of such an event In [1CO 3:9](https://ref.ly/Rom3:9) the phrase θεοῦ οἰκοδομή has been interpreted by many as ‘a dwelling place for God’ (see [7\.1](#7.1)), but it is more likely that the Christian is regarded as the result of God’s activity, that is to say, the believer is ‘God’s construction’ or ‘that which God has made.’ οἰκοδομή in [2CO 5:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:1) (οἰκοδομὴν ἐκ θεοῦ ἔχομεν ‘we have a construction from God’) may be regarded as having essentially the same meaning as in [1CO 3:9](https://ref.ly/Rom3:9), though the reference here is to the glorified body.

[1CO 3:9](https://ref.ly/Rom3:9) · [2CO 5:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:1) · [EPH 4:12](https://ref.ly/Gal4:12) · [EPH 4:16](https://ref.ly/Gal4:16) · [EPH 4:29](https://ref.ly/Gal4:29)

74\.15 · Able, Capable

to strengthen, to make more able, to build up to increase the potential of someone or something, with focus upon the process involved

[ROM 14:19](https://ref.ly/Acts14:19) · [ROM 15:2](https://ref.ly/Acts15:2) · [1CO 14:3](https://ref.ly/Rom14:3) · [1CO 14:5](https://ref.ly/Rom14:5) · [1CO 14:12](https://ref.ly/Rom14:12) · [1CO 14:26](https://ref.ly/Rom14:26) · [2CO 10:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:8) · [2CO 12:19](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:19) · [2CO 13:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:10)

1 οἰκοδομή\[a] may be interpreted as a derivative of οἰκοδομέω\[a] to construct ([45\.1](#45.1)), or the latter may be interpreted as a causative derivative of οἰκοδομή\[a], with the meaning to cause a building to exist. In view of the highly generic nature of οἰκοδομή\[a], it is perhaps better to interpret it as a derivative of the corresponding event expression ([45\.1](#45.1)) since this seems to be the predominant pattern of derivative correspondences. But because of the obvious material form of constructions as buildings, οἰκοδομή\[a] is introduced into Domain 7 as a generic for all the other meanings.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:1; Mark 13:1; Mark 13:2; Ephesians 2:21; 1 Corinthians 3:9; 2 Corinthians 5:1; Ephesians 4:12; Ephesians 4:16; Ephesians 4:29; Romans 14:19; Romans 15:2; 1 Corinthians 14:3; 1 Corinthians 14:5; 1 Corinthians 14:12; 1 Corinthians 14:26; 2 Corinthians 10:8; 2 Corinthians 12:19; 2 Corinthians 13:10

## οἰκονομέω (id: 003525000000000)

οἰκονομέω
=========

οἰκονομέω (G3621)

verb \| οἰκέω \+ νομίζω

46\.1 · Household Activities

to manage a household, to run a household, to be in charge of a household to manage and provide for a household In biblical times a household would consist of more than the nuclear family, that is to say, more than simply husband, wife, and children, for servants, slaves, and permanent hired workers living with the family would be regarded as a part of the total household.

[LUK 16:2](https://ref.ly/Mark16:2)

task, commission, responsibility a task involving management and organization

purpose, scheme, plan, arrangement a plan which involves a set of arrangements (referring in the NT to God’s plan for bringing salvation to mankind within the course of history)

to manage a household, to run a household, to be in charge of a household to manage and provide for a household In biblical times a household would consist of more than the nuclear family, that is to say, more than simply husband, wife, and children, for servants, slaves, and permanent hired workers living with the family would be regarded as a part of the total household.

οἰκονόμος (G3623)

manager of a household, steward one who is in charge of running a household It may be necessary in some languages to indicate that οἰκονόμος\[a] was a person hired to engage in a particular activity, and therefore in [LUK 16:1](https://ref.ly/Mark16:1) it may be necessary to say ‘there was a rich man who had hired a man to manage his household.’

one who is in charge of, one who is responsible for, adminstrator, manager one who has the authority and responsibility for something

57\.231 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Keep Records

city treasurer (an idiom, literally: manager of a city) one who is in charge of the finances of a city

## οἶκος (id: 003528000000000)

οἶκος
=====

οἶκος (G3624)

noun, m. \| \-ου \| οἰκέω

7\.2 · Constructions · Buildings

house, temple, sanctuary a building consisting of one or more rooms and normally serving as a dwelling place (οἶκος\[a] also includes certain public buildings, for example, a temple. The extension of οἶκος\[a] to include temples may be the result of speaking of a temple as the dwelling place of the deity.) For a discussion of certain translational problems related to οἶκος\[a], see [7\.3](#7.3).

3:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:5) · [HEB 10:21](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:21) · [1PE 2:5](https://ref.ly/Jas2:5)

10\.8 · Kinship Terms · Groups and Members of Groups of Persons Regarded as Related by Blood but without Special Reference to Successive Generations

family, household the family consisting of those related by blood and marriage, as well as slaves and servants, living in the same house or homestead In a number of languages the equivalent of οἶκος\[b] or οἰκία\[b] would be ‘those who live together’ or ‘those who have the same fence’ (this being a reference to a group of huts surrounded by a fence and thus constituting a single so\-called ‘family unit’).  
In [ACT 7:10](https://ref.ly/John7:10) οἶκος can mean the household over which Joseph was placed, or it can mean the property of Pharaoh (see [57\.21](#57.21)).

[LUK 10:5](https://ref.ly/Mark10:5) · [LUK 11:17](https://ref.ly/Mark11:17) · [LUK 11:17](https://ref.ly/Mark11:17) · [LUK 12:52](https://ref.ly/Mark12:52) · [LUK 16:27](https://ref.ly/Mark16:27) · [LUK 19:9](https://ref.ly/Mark19:9) · [ACT 7:10](https://ref.ly/John7:10) · [ACT 7:20](https://ref.ly/John7:20) · [ACT 10:2](https://ref.ly/John10:2) · [ACT 11:14](https://ref.ly/John11:14) · [ACT 16:15](https://ref.ly/John16:15) · [ACT 16:31](https://ref.ly/John16:31) · [ACT 18:8](https://ref.ly/John18:8) · [1CO 1:16](https://ref.ly/Rom1:16) · [1TI 3:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:4) · [1TI 3:5](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:5) · [1TI 3:12](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:12) · [1TI 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:15) · [1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4) · [2TI 1:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:16) · [2TI 4:19](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:19) · [TIT 1:11](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:11) · [HEB 3:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:2) · [HEB 3:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:6) · [HEB 3:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:6) · [HEB 11:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:7) · [1PE 4:17](https://ref.ly/Jas4:17)

10\.24 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

lineage, family line persons of successive generations who are related by birth In biblical times the reckoning of a lineage was somewhat complex in view of certain distinctions between legal and biological relations. The focus of meaning in γένεσις\[b], οἶκος\[c], and πατριά\[a] is not, however, the list of ancestors, but the lineage of successively related persons to which the reference person belongs. Some scholars, however, interpret γένεσις\[c] history, [33\.19](#33.19)).  
In some languages a successive series of ancestors is called ‘the forefathers’ or ‘those to whom one looks back’ or ‘those who have gone ahead.’ In other languages a lineage is referred to as ‘a person’s root’ or ‘a man’s trunk’ (referring to the trunk of a tree).  
In [LUK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Mark2:4) there appears to be no significant difference in meaning between οἶκος and πατριά, and in some receptor languages it would be sufficient to use a single term essentially equivalent to ‘lineage.’ The two terms appear to simply reinforce their shared meaning.

[LUK 1:27](https://ref.ly/Mark1:27) · [LUK 1:33](https://ref.ly/Mark1:33) · [LUK 1:69](https://ref.ly/Mark1:69) · [LUK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Mark2:4)

57\.21 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Have, Possess, Property, Owner

property, possessions (figurative extension of meaning of οἶκος\[a] ‘house,’ [7\.2](#7.2)) possessions associated with a house and household The rendering ‘property’ seems to be far more justified by the context and by general usage than the more common meaning of ‘household’ (see [10\.8](#10.8)).

[ACT 7:10](https://ref.ly/John7:10)

11\.58 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

**Collocation:** οἶκος Ἰσραήλ · οἶκος Ἰούδα

the people of Israel, the nation of Israel (idioms, literally: house of Israel, and: house of Judah, respectively) the people of Israel as an ethnic entity In translating the expression ‘the people of Israel’ it may be necessary to use an appositional phrase, for example, ‘the people, namely, Israel’ or a relative clause which also serves an appositional function, for example, ‘the people who are Israel.’ In some languages it may be preferable to use a term such as ‘nation’ followed by some expression which will indicate that Israel is simply the name of the nation, for example, ‘the nation called Israel.’

Temple a building consisting of one or more rooms and serving as a place of worship

## οἰκουμένη (id: 003529000000000)

οἰκουμένη
=========

οἰκουμένη (G3625)

noun, f. \| \-ης \| οἰκέω

1\.39 · Geographical Objects and Features · The Earth's Surface

earth, world the surface of the earth as the dwelling place of mankind, in contrast with the heavens above and the world below1 In [HEB 2:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:5) there is a reference to τὴν οἰκουμένην τὴν μέλλουσαν ‘the world to come, the future world.’ This has generally been interpreted as being equivalent to ‘the coming age,’ but the use of οἰκουμένη would seem to imply an inhabited world.

[MAT 24:14](https://ref.ly/Matt24:14) · [LUK 4:5](https://ref.ly/Mark4:5) · [LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26) · [ACT 11:28](https://ref.ly/John11:28) · [ROM 10:18](https://ref.ly/Acts10:18) · [HEB 1:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:6) · [HEB 2:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:5) · [REV 3:10](https://ref.ly/Jude3:10) · [REV 12:9](https://ref.ly/Jude12:9) · [REV 16:14](https://ref.ly/Jude16:14)

1\.83 · Geographical Objects and Features · Governmental Administrative Areas

empire, people of the empire the Roman Empire, including its inhabitants (οἰκουμένη\[b] differs from οἰκουμένη\[a] ‘earth,’ [1\.39](#1.39), in that those outside of the Roman Empire are specifically excluded, that is to say, no census was to be taken of the barbarians, but only of the inhabitants of the Empire) In order to represent accurately the meaning of οἰκουμένη\[b] in [LUK 2:1](https://ref.ly/Mark2:1) it may be possible to translate ‘Emperor Augustus sent out an order that all the people over whom he ruled should register themselves for the census.’

[LUK 2:1](https://ref.ly/Mark2:1)2 · [ACT 24:5](https://ref.ly/John24:5)

9\.22 · People · Human Beings

people, all mankind (figurative extension of meaning of οἰκουμένη\[a] ‘inhabited earth,’ [1\.39](#1.39)) all people who dwell on the earth

[MAT 24:14](https://ref.ly/Matt24:14) · [ACT 11:28](https://ref.ly/John11:28) · [ACT 17:6](https://ref.ly/John17:6) · [ACT 17:31](https://ref.ly/John17:31) · [ACT 19:27](https://ref.ly/John19:27)

1 The occurrences of γῆ\[a], οἰκουμένη\[a], and κόσμος\[b] relate specifically to the known inhabited areas of the earth, which in the NT period included the countries bordering on the Mediterranean, the Fertile Crescent as far as the Indus Valley, and as far north in Europe as England and Scandinavia. This was the total known earth or world. Such a restriction to a known area of the earth is also true of terms used in some receptor languages, though the referents are obviously different. What is important is not the particular area of the earth, but the corresponding concept of the surface of the earth as the place of human habitation. It is possible that οἰκουμένη\[a] differs slightly in meaning from γῆ\[a] and κόσμος\[b] in view of a somewhat greater emphasis or focus upon the civilized part of earth. 2 LUK 2:1 In [LUK 2:1](https://ref.ly/Mark2:1) οἰκουμένη\[b] refers specifically to the inhabitants of the empire, though the meaning of οἰκουμένη\[b] is simply the Roman Empire as a region, a governmental entity, and a population.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:14; Luke 4:5; Luke 21:26; Acts 11:28; Romans 10:18; Hebrews 1:6; Hebrews 2:5; Revelation 3:10; Revelation 12:9; Revelation 16:14; Luke 2:1; Acts 24:5; Acts 17:6; Acts 17:31; Acts 19:27

## οἶνος (id: 003535000000000)

οἶνος
=====

οἶνος (G3631)

noun, m. \| \-ου \| οἰνοπότης · οἰνοφλυγία · πάροινος

6\.197 · Artifacts · Plant Products

wine a fermented beverage made from the juice of grapes1 Though some persons have argued that whenever mention is made of Jesus either making or drinking wine, one must assume that this was only unfermented grape juice, there is no real basis for such a conclusion. Only where οἶνος νέος ‘new wine’ ([6\.198](#6.198)) is mentioned can one assume that this is unfermented grape juice or grape juice in the initial stages of fermentation.  
In a number of languages there is no indigenous term for wine, and some expression may simply be borrowed from a dominant language. On the other hand, it is sometimes possible to employ a descriptive phrase, for example, ‘fermented fruit juice.’ In some languages the equivalent for ‘wine’ is more specifically ‘palm wine,’ that is to say, a wine made from the sap of certain palm trees. Such a term may also have a more generic meaning and be applicable to any kind of wine.  
There are a number of passages in the NT where one must be particularly careful in the selection of terms to translate wine. For example, in [EPH 5:18](https://ref.ly/Gal5:18) a literal translation of ‘do not get drunk with wine’ could be interpreted to mean that it is permissible for one to get drunk on other types of intoxicating liquors. It may, therefore, be necessary in some languages to render [EPH 5:18](https://ref.ly/Gal5:18) as simply ‘do not get drunk.’  
In [LUK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Mark10:34) (κατέδησεν τὰ τραύματα αὐτοῦ ἐπιχέων ἔλαιον καὶ οἶνον ‘he poured on olive oil and wine and bandaged his wounds’) it is important to recognize the medicinal value of oil and wine. In some instances translators have indicated the purpose of such an activity by translating ‘to cleanse and heal his wounds he poured wine and oil on them and bandaged them.’ It may also be possible to add in a marginal note information as to the antiseptic quality of the wine and the value of the oil in the healing process.2

[MAT 9:17](https://ref.ly/Matt9:17) · [MAT 27:34](https://ref.ly/Matt27:34) · [MRK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [LUK 1:15](https://ref.ly/Mark1:15) · [LUK 7:33](https://ref.ly/Mark7:33) · [LUK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Mark10:34) · [JHN 2:3](https://ref.ly/Luke2:3) · [JHN 2:3](https://ref.ly/Luke2:3) · [JHN 2:9](https://ref.ly/Luke2:9) · [JHN 2:10](https://ref.ly/Luke2:10) · [JHN 2:10](https://ref.ly/Luke2:10) · [JHN 4:46](https://ref.ly/Luke4:46) · [ROM 14:21](https://ref.ly/Acts14:21) · [EPH 5:18](https://ref.ly/Gal5:18) · [1TI 3:8](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:8) · [1TI 5:23](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:23) · [TIT 2:3](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:3) · [REV 6:6](https://ref.ly/Jude6:6) · [REV 14:8](https://ref.ly/Jude14:8) · [REV 14:10](https://ref.ly/Jude14:10) · [REV 16:19](https://ref.ly/Jude16:19) · [REV 17:2](https://ref.ly/Jude17:2) · [REV 18:3](https://ref.ly/Jude18:3) · [REV 18:13](https://ref.ly/Jude18:13) · [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15)

6\.198 · Artifacts · Plant Products

**Collocation:** οἶνος νέος

new wine, grape juice a set phrase referring to newly pressed grape juice, unfermented or in the initial stages of fermentation

[MAT 9:17](https://ref.ly/Matt9:17) · [MAT 9:17](https://ref.ly/Matt9:17) · [MRK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [MRK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [MRK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [LUK 5:37](https://ref.ly/Mark5:37) · [LUK 5:37](https://ref.ly/Mark5:37) · [LUK 5:38](https://ref.ly/Mark5:38)

6\.204 · Artifacts · Medicines

**Collocation:** ἐσμυρνισμένος οἶνος

myrrhed wine a set phrase referring to wine drugged with myrrh and used as a stupefying potion3 In general, ἐσμυρνισμένος οἶνος must be rendered by means of a phrase or a clause, for example, ‘wine which has been mixed with a drug called myrrh’ or ‘wine mixed with a drug to reduce pain.’ It is, of course, also possible to use an abbreviated phrase such as ‘myrrhed wine’ and then to place the explanation of its function in a marginal note.

[MRK 15:23](https://ref.ly/Matt15:23)

1 It is possible that the phrase γένημα τῆς ἀμπέλου product of the grapevine ([MAT 26:29](https://ref.ly/Matt26:29)) should be clssified as a fixed phrase meaning wine, but it is also possible to treat this phrase as semantically endocentric (that is, non\-idiomatic), in which case the reference could potentially be either grapes or wine. 2 Though the various terms for wine and olive oil might very well be treated simply in the category of Food (Domain 5\), they obviously served other functions as well, for wine was used medicinally as an antiseptic and olive oil was used not only for food but also for illumination in lamps. Since both the production of wine and olive oil involved relatively elaborate processes, it therefore seems best to classify them as artifacts rather than subsume them under various functional categories. 3 The term ἐσμυρνισμένος οἶνος can be seen as semantically endocentric (σμυρνίζω, ‘mixed with myrrh’ and οἶνος, ‘wine’). However, due to its specific role in the passage of [MRK 15:23](https://ref.ly/Matt15:23), it was included in this semantic field.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 9:17; Matthew 27:34; Mark 2:22; Luke 1:15; Luke 7:33; Luke 10:34; John 2:3; John 2:9; John 2:10; John 4:46; Romans 14:21; Ephesians 5:18; 1 Timothy 3:8; 1 Timothy 5:23; Titus 2:3; Revelation 6:6; Revelation 14:8; Revelation 14:10; Revelation 16:19; Revelation 17:2; Revelation 18:3; Revelation 18:13; Revelation 19:15; Luke 5:37; Luke 5:38; Mark 15:23

ὀλίγος
======

ὀλίγος (G3641)

adjc. \| \-η, ον \| ὀλιγωρέω

59\.3 · Quantity · Many, Few \[Countables]

few, less a relatively small quantity on any dimension

[MAT 7:14](https://ref.ly/Matt7:14) · [MAT 9:37](https://ref.ly/Matt9:37) · [MAT 15:34](https://ref.ly/Matt15:34) · [MAT 22:14](https://ref.ly/Matt22:14) · [MAT 25:21](https://ref.ly/Matt25:21) · [MAT 25:23](https://ref.ly/Matt25:23) · [MRK 6:5](https://ref.ly/Matt6:5) · [MRK 8:7](https://ref.ly/Matt8:7) · [LUK 10:2](https://ref.ly/Mark10:2) · [LUK 12:48](https://ref.ly/Mark12:48) · [LUK 13:23](https://ref.ly/Mark13:23) · [ACT 17:4](https://ref.ly/John17:4) · [ACT 17:12](https://ref.ly/John17:12) · [1PE 3:20](https://ref.ly/Jas3:20) · [REV 2:14](https://ref.ly/Jude2:14) · [REV 3:4](https://ref.ly/Jude3:4)

78\.8 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

little, to a small degree, slight a relatively low point on a scale of extent The first ὀλίγον in [LUK 7:47](https://ref.ly/Mark7:47) is an expression of quantity (see [59\.13](#59.13)), while the second ὀλίγον indicates degree. Accordingly, in some languages it may be necessary to translate [LUK 7:47](https://ref.ly/Mark7:47) as ‘but whoever has been forgiven only a few things loves only a little bit.’  
In a number of languages, however, it is impossible to speak of ‘not a little’ (a case of litotes or purposeful understatement). The appropriate equivalent in [ACT 12:18](https://ref.ly/John12:18) would be simply ‘there was great confusion among the guards’ or ‘the guards were intensely disturbed’ or ‘the guards were in tremendous confusion.’

[LUK 7:47](https://ref.ly/Mark7:47) · [ACT 12:18](https://ref.ly/John12:18) · [ACT 15:2](https://ref.ly/John15:2) · [ACT 19:23](https://ref.ly/John19:23) · [ACT 27:20](https://ref.ly/John27:20) · [1TI 4:8](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:8)

59\.13 · Quantity · Much, Little \[Masses, Collectives, Extensions]

little, small amount a relatively small quantity

[MRK 1:19](https://ref.ly/Matt1:19) · [MRK 6:31](https://ref.ly/Matt6:31) · [LUK 5:3](https://ref.ly/Mark5:3) · [LUK 7:47](https://ref.ly/Mark7:47) · [ACT 14:28](https://ref.ly/John14:28) · [ACT 19:24](https://ref.ly/John19:24) · [2CO 8:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:15) · [1TI 5:23](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:23) · [JAS 4:14](https://ref.ly/Heb4:14) · [REV 12:12](https://ref.ly/Jude12:12)

67\.106 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** δι᾽ ὀλίγων · ἐν ὀλίγῳ · πρὸς ὀλίγον

a little while, for a little while, a short time, brief, briefly pertaining to a relatively brief extent of time1 It is also possible to interpret ἐν ὀλίγῳ in [ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28) as meaning ‘easily’ or ‘without difficulty’ (see [22\.41](#22.41)).  
In some languages it may be best to translate the phrase in [1PE 5:12](https://ref.ly/Jas5:12) as ‘I have written a brief letter.’

[ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28) · [ACT 26:29](https://ref.ly/John26:29) · [EPH 3:3](https://ref.ly/Gal3:3) · [HEB 12:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:10) · [JAS 4:14](https://ref.ly/Heb4:14) · [1PE 1:6](https://ref.ly/Jas1:6) · [1PE 5:10](https://ref.ly/Jas5:10) · [1PE 5:12](https://ref.ly/Jas5:12) · [REV 17:10](https://ref.ly/Jude17:10)

1 The series of expressions indicating relative shortness of time may very well differ slightly, at least in connotative meaning, but on the basis of existing NT contexts it is not possible to determine significant denotative differences. The phrases in this series may be technically regarded as idioms, but functionally they are better classed as frozen phrases, which act essentially like morphological units.

to look down on, to have contempt for, to make light of, to despise to regard something or someone as of little value

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 12:5


## ὀλίγως (id: 003549000000000)

ὀλίγως
======

ὀλίγως

advb.

78\.41 · Degree · About, Approximately, Almost, Hardly \[Approximate Degree]

barely, just, scarcely, with difficulty, hardly a degree which almost equals some point on a scale of extent, but not quite In a number of languages it may be difficult to render the degree ‘which falls just short of the precise extent.’ For example, in the case of [2PE 2:18](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:18) an equivalent of τοὺς ὀλίγως ἀποφεύγοντας (GNT4\) may be ‘those who were just starting to escape.’

==========

noun, m. \| \-οῦ \| ὀλοθρεύω

destroyer (derivative of ὀλοθρεύω ‘to destroy, to ruin,’ [20\.34](#20.34)) one who causes destruction In [1CO 10:10](https://ref.ly/Rom10:10) ὀλοθρευτής may serve as a title referring to a destroying angel or even to Satan.

[1CO 10:10](https://ref.ly/Rom10:10)

=========

noun, f. \| \-ας \| ὅλος \+ κληρόω

23\.131 · Physiological Processes and States · Health, Vigor, Strength

complete health, perfect health a state of complete health or soundness in all parts of the body In some languages it may be possible to translate ὁλοκληρίαν as ‘very healthy’ or ‘all parts of his body are healthy’ or ‘... strong.’

=========

adjc. \| \-ον \| ὅλος \+ κληρόω

59\.30 · Quantity · All, Any, Each, Every \[Totality]

whole, entire a totality, with special emphasis upon the entity as a whole In a number of languages it may be difficult to speak of ‘your entire being.’ A substitute expression in [1TH 5:23](https://ref.ly/Col5:23) may be ‘yourselves as one person.’

## ὀλολύζω (id: 003555000000000)

ὀλολύζω
=======

ὀλολύζω (G3649)

verb

25\.140 · Attitudes and Emotions · Laugh, Cry, Groan

to make a loud cry, to cry aloud to make a loud cry as an expression of either joy or sorrow In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘cry aloud’ would simply be ‘scream.’ In this one occurrence of ὀλολύζω in the NT, the context refers to desperate circumstances, and one must often select an expression for ‘screaming’ or ‘crying’ which will be in keeping with this context referring to the threat of future misery.

[JAS 5:1](https://ref.ly/Heb5:1)

* **Associated Passages:** James 5:1

=======

## ὄλυνθος (id: 003559000000000)

ὄλυνθος
=======

ὄλυνθος (G3653)

noun, m. \| \-ου

3\.37 · Plants · Fruit Parts of Plants

late fig fruit of the fig tree συκῆ, produced late in the summer season (and often falling off before it ripens) In the only context in which ὄλυνθος occurs in the NT ([REV 6:13](https://ref.ly/Jude6:13)), one may employ an expression such as ‘unripe fig’ or ‘fig which ripens late.’

[REV 6:13](https://ref.ly/Jude6:13)

## ὅλως (id: 003560000000000)

ὅλως
====

ὅλως (G3654)

advb. \| ὅλος

78\.44 · Degree · Completely, Enough \[Completive Degree]

complete, completely, totally, totality a degree of totality or completeness1 In [MAT 5:34](https://ref.ly/Matt5:34) ὅλως should not be interpreted as a qualifier of ὀμόσαι ‘to swear,’ for this is not a matter of ‘not swearing completely,’ but ὅλως is a degree attributive of the proposition ‘do not swear.’ In a number of languages the closest equivalent is ‘do not ever swear’ or ‘under no circumstances whatsoever make an oath.’

[MAT 5:34](https://ref.ly/Matt5:34) · [1CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/Rom6:7)

70\.1 · Real, Unreal

actually, really pertaining to reality, with the implication of being generally known2

[MAT 5:34](https://ref.ly/Matt5:34) · [1CO 5:1](https://ref.ly/Rom5:1) · [1CO 15:29](https://ref.ly/Rom15:29)

1 There may very well be certain significant differences of meaning in this series of degree expressions, but this is difficult, if not impossible, to determine from existing contexts. 2 It would seem that the meanings of items [70\.1](#70.1)\-[70\.4](#70.4) do reflect certain subtle differences, but it is not at all easy to determine precisely how they differ.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:34; 1 Corinthians 6:7; 1 Corinthians 5:1; 1 Corinthians 15:29


## ὄμβρος (id: 003561000000000)

ὄμβρος
======

ὄμβρος (G3655)

noun, m. \| \-ου

14\.12 · Physical Events and States · Rain

rainstorm, thunderstorm a heavy rain, either in length of occurrence or in force, as in the case of a thunderstorm (thus differing from ὑετός\[b], βροχή and βρέχω\[a] in [14\.10](#14.10)) In some languages a rainstorm is simply ‘a rain with wind,’ but it may also be expressed as ‘rain that hurts’ or ‘rain that floods.’

[LUK 12:54](https://ref.ly/Mark12:54)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 12:54


## ὁμείρομαι (id: 003562000000000)

ὁμείρομαι
=========

ὁμείρομαι (G2442a)

verb

25\.47 · Attitudes and Emotions · Love, Affection, Compassion

to have a great affection for, to have a yearning love for to experience a yearning affection for someone The nominal phrase with the English term ‘affection’ can and must be expressed in a number of languages by verb phrases or clauses. In [1TH 2:8](https://ref.ly/Col2:8) one may translate ‘because we love you so much, we were ready to share with you.’

[1TH 2:8](https://ref.ly/Col2:8)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Thessalonians 2:8


## ὁμιλέω (id: 003563000000000)

ὁμιλέω
======

ὁμιλέω (G3656)

verb \| ὁμοιόω \+ εἴλω

33\.156 · Communication · Converse, Discuss

to talk (with), to speak (with) to speak with someone, with the implication of a reversal of roles in communication

[LUK 24:14](https://ref.ly/Mark24:14)1 · [LUK 24:15](https://ref.ly/Mark24:15) · [ACT 20:11](https://ref.ly/John20:11) · [ACT 24:26](https://ref.ly/John24:26)

1 LUK 24:14 The reciprocity expressed in [LUK 24:14](https://ref.ly/Mark24:14) is indicated directly by the reciprocal pronouns.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 24:14; Luke 24:15; Acts 20:11; Acts 24:26


## ὁμιλία (id: 003564000000000)

ὁμιλία
======

ὁμιλία (G3657)

noun, f. \| \-ας \| ὁμιλέω

34\.1 · Association · Associate

to associate, to be in the company of, to be involved with, association to associate with one another, normally involving spacial proximity and/or joint activity, and usually implying some kind of reciprocal relation or involvement In translating terms referring to association, one may employ a number of different kinds of expressions, for example, ‘to have something to do with,’ ‘to keep company with,’ ‘to go around with,’ ‘to join in doing things together,’ or ‘to become a companion of.’ Sometimes association is spoken of in terms of the impression made upon others, for example, ‘to be seen often together,’ ‘to be regarded as close friends,’ and even idiomatically as ‘to be another person’s shadow.’

[1CO 15:33](https://ref.ly/Rom15:33)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 15:33


## ὅμιλος (id: 003565000000000)

ὅμιλος
======

ὅμιλος

verb \| \-ου \| ὁμιλέω

11\.1 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · General

crowd, multitude a casual non\-membership group of people, fairly large in size and assembled for whatever purpose1 In a number of languages there is no term corresponding to ‘crowd.’ One may, however, usually speak of such a group of people as ‘many people’ or ‘many men and women.’ Depending on the context, it may besary to indicate some relative difference in size, for example, ‘very large crowd’ or ‘many, many people.’ If the crowd is relatively small, one may sometimes speak of ‘just some people.’  
This word is found as a variant reading only: πᾶς ἐπὶ τῶν πλοίων ὁ ὅμιλος ‘all the crowd in ships’ [REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17) (apparatus).

[REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17)

1 In this set of terms referring to a crowd or a multitude, it is possible that πλῆθος\[b] denotes a somewhat larger group of persons than the other terms and that λαός\[c] suggests a crowd made up of common people, but one cannot be certain of these distinctions from existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 18:17


## ὁμίχλη (id: 003566000000000)

ὁμίχλη
======

ὁμίχλη (G3507a)

noun, f. \| \-ης

1\.35 · Geographical Objects and Features · Atmospheric Objects

fog, mist a cloud\-like mass of moisture close to the earth's surface In the NT ὁμίχλη is used only figuratively: οὗτοί εἰσιν πηγαὶ ἄνυδροι καὶ ὁμίχλαι ὑπὸ λαίλαπος ἐλαυνόμεναι ‘these men are dried up springs or mists blown along by a storm’ [2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17). The metaphorical usage in [2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17) must be marked in a number of languages as a simile, for example, ‘these men are like springs that have dried up and are like mists which the storm blows along.’

[2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 2:17


## ὄμμα (id: 003567000000000)

ὄμμα
====

ὄμμα (G3659)

noun, n. \| \-τος \| ὁράω

8\.23 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

eye (normally including the eyelids) the organ of sight in humans and many animals, occurring in a pair in the face or elsewhere on the head In a number of languages an important distinction is made between the eyes consisting of the eyeballs and the eyes covered or partially covered by the eyelids. In [MAT 9:29](https://ref.ly/Matt9:29) and [MAT 20:34](https://ref.ly/Matt20:34), Jesus apparently touched the eyelids, not the eyeballs.

[MAT 20:34](https://ref.ly/Matt20:34) · [MRK 8:23](https://ref.ly/Matt8:23)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 20:34; Mark 8:23


## ὀμνύω (id: 003568000000000)

ὀμνύω
=====

ὀμνύω (G3660)

verb \| inf. ὀμνύναι · aor. ὤμοσα · inf. ὀμόσαι \| ὁρκωμοσία · συνωμοσία

33\.463 · Communication · Swear, Put Under Oath, Vow

to swear, to make an oath, oath to affirm the truth of a statement by calling on a divine being to execute sanctions against a person if the statement in question is not true (in the case of a deity taking an oath, his divine being is regarded as validating the statement) In a number of languages it is necessary to be quite specific in referring to the swearing of an oath, for example, ‘to say something by calling upon God to listen’ or ‘to state that something is true and asking God to punish if it is not true’ or ‘to make God responsible for what one has said.’  
For [MRK 14:71](https://ref.ly/Matt14:71) see [33\.472](#33.472).

[MAT 5:34](https://ref.ly/Matt5:34) · [MAT 5:36](https://ref.ly/Matt5:36) · [MAT 23:16](https://ref.ly/Matt23:16) · [MAT 23:16](https://ref.ly/Matt23:16) · [MAT 23:18](https://ref.ly/Matt23:18) · [MAT 23:18](https://ref.ly/Matt23:18) · [MAT 23:20](https://ref.ly/Matt23:20) · [MAT 23:20](https://ref.ly/Matt23:20) · [MAT 23:21](https://ref.ly/Matt23:21) · [MAT 23:21](https://ref.ly/Matt23:21) · [MAT 23:22](https://ref.ly/Matt23:22) · [MAT 23:22](https://ref.ly/Matt23:22) · [MAT 26:74](https://ref.ly/Matt26:74) · [MRK 6:23](https://ref.ly/Matt6:23) · [MRK 14:71](https://ref.ly/Matt14:71) · [LUK 1:73](https://ref.ly/Mark1:73) · [ACT 2:30](https://ref.ly/John2:30) · [HEB 3:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:11) · [HEB 3:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:18) · [HEB 4:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:3) · [HEB 6:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:13) · [HEB 6:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:13) · [HEB 6:16](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:16) · [HEB 7:21](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:21) · [JAS 5:12](https://ref.ly/Heb5:12) · [REV 10:6](https://ref.ly/Jude10:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:34; Matthew 5:36; Matthew 23:16; Matthew 23:18; Matthew 23:20; Matthew 23:21; Matthew 23:22; Matthew 26:74; Mark 6:23; Mark 14:71; Luke 1:73; Acts 2:30; Hebrews 3:11; Hebrews 3:18; Hebrews 4:3; Hebrews 6:13; Hebrews 6:16; Hebrews 7:21; James 5:12; Revelation 10:6


## ὁμοθυμαδόν (id: 003569000000000)

ὁμοθυμαδόν
==========

ὁμοθυμαδόν (G3661)

advb. \| ὁμοιόω \+ θυμόομαι

31\.23 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Agree, Consent

with one mind, by common consent, unanimously pertaining to mutual consent or agreement In a number of languages with one mind ([ACT 1:14](https://ref.ly/John1:14)) would need to be rendered as ‘prayed for the same things’ or ‘said the same in their prayers.’  
In [ACT 15:25](https://ref.ly/John15:25) the expression ‘we unanimously agreed’ may also be rendered as ‘we were in agreement that.’

[ACT 1:14](https://ref.ly/John1:14) · [ACT 2:46](https://ref.ly/John2:46) · [ACT 4:24](https://ref.ly/John4:24) · [ACT 5:12](https://ref.ly/John5:12) · [ACT 7:57](https://ref.ly/John7:57) · [ACT 8:6](https://ref.ly/John8:6) · [ACT 12:20](https://ref.ly/John12:20) · [ACT 15:25](https://ref.ly/John15:25) · [ACT 18:12](https://ref.ly/John18:12) · [ACT 19:29](https://ref.ly/John19:29) · [ROM 15:6](https://ref.ly/Acts15:6)

## ὁμοιόω (id: 003573000000000)

ὁμοιόω
======

ὁμοιόω (G3666)

verb \| ἀνθομολογέομαι · ἀφομοιόω · ἐξομολογέω · ὁμιλέω · ὁμιλία · ὅμιλος · ὁμοθυμαδόν · ὁμοιάζω · ὁμοιοπαθής · ὅμοιος · ὁμοιότης · ὁμοίωμα · ὁμοίως · ὁμοίωσις · ὁμολογέω · ὁμολογία · ὁμολογουμένως · ὁμότεχνος · ὁμοῦ · ὁμόφρων · ὅμως · παροιμία · παρομοιάζω · παρόμοιος

64\.4 · Comparison

to be like, to resemble, to be similar to to be like or similar to something else

[MAT 6:8](https://ref.ly/Matt6:8) · [MAT 7:24](https://ref.ly/Matt7:24) · [MAT 7:26](https://ref.ly/Matt7:26) · [MAT 13:24](https://ref.ly/Matt13:24) · [MAT 18:23](https://ref.ly/Matt18:23) · [MAT 22:2](https://ref.ly/Matt22:2) · [ACT 14:11](https://ref.ly/John14:11) · [ROM 9:29](https://ref.ly/Acts9:29) · [HEB 2:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:17)

64\.5 · Comparison

to compare to consider something to be like something else1

[MAT 11:16](https://ref.ly/Matt11:16) · [MAT 25:1](https://ref.ly/Matt25:1) · [MRK 4:30](https://ref.ly/Matt4:30) · [LUK 7:31](https://ref.ly/Mark7:31) · [LUK 13:18](https://ref.ly/Mark13:18) · [LUK 13:20](https://ref.ly/Mark13:20)

1 In a number of languages there is relatively little difficulty in finding an adequate lexical expression for being like or being similar to; however, it is often much more difficult to find a satisfactory equivalent for the process of comparing. In many instances the equivalent expression is somewhat paraphrastic, for example, to see things together, to set things alongside of one another, to measure things against one another, or to say how similar things are.

## ὁμολογέω (id: 003577000000000)

ὁμολογέω
========

ὁμολογέω (G3670)

verb \| ὁμοιόω \+ λέγω

33\.274 · Communication · Profess Allegiance

to profess, to confess, confession to express openly one’s allegiance to a proposition or person It is often extremely difficult, if not impossible, to translate ὁμολογέω\[a], ὁμολογία, and ἐξομολογέω\[b] by the usual expression for ‘confess,’ since this would usually imply that one has done something wrong. It is normally necessary, therefore, to employ quite a different type of relationship, usually involving a public utterance and an expression of confidence or allegiance. For example, in [MAT 10:32](https://ref.ly/Matt10:32) it may be necessary to translate ‘whoever tells people publicly that he is loyal to me, I will tell my Father that I am loyal to that person.’ Similarly, in [HEB 10:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:23) one may translate ‘let us hold on to the hope in which we have told people we have such confidence.’ Likewise, in [ROM 15:9](https://ref.ly/Acts15:9) one may translate ‘therefore I will tell the Gentiles how I have put my confidence in you.’

[MAT 10:32](https://ref.ly/Matt10:32) · [MAT 10:32](https://ref.ly/Matt10:32) · [LUK 12:8](https://ref.ly/Mark12:8) · [LUK 12:8](https://ref.ly/Mark12:8) · [JHN 9:22](https://ref.ly/Luke9:22) · [JHN 12:42](https://ref.ly/Luke12:42) · [ACT 23:8](https://ref.ly/John23:8) · [ROM 10:9](https://ref.ly/Acts10:9) · [ROM 10:10](https://ref.ly/Acts10:10) · [1TI 6:12](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:12) · [TIT 1:16](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:16) · [1JN 2:23](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:23) · [1JN 4:2](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:2) · [1JN 4:3](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:3) · [1JN 4:15](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:15) · [2JN 1:7](https://ref.ly/1John1:7) · [REV 3:5](https://ref.ly/Jude3:5)

33\.275 · Communication · Admit, Confess, Deny

to admit, to confess to acknowledge a fact publicly, often in reference to previous bad behavior In [PHP 2:11](https://ref.ly/Eph2:11) the statement πᾶσα γλῶσσα ἐξομολογήσηται ὅτι κύριος Ἰησοῦς Χριστός (‘that everyone may confess that Jesus Christ is Lord’) means simply to acknowledge a fact publicly, and in this instance there is no implication of previous bad behavior.

[JHN 1:20](https://ref.ly/Luke1:20) · [ACT 24:14](https://ref.ly/John24:14) · [HEB 11:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:13) · [1JN 1:9](https://ref.ly/2Pet1:9)

33\.221 · Communication · Assert, Declare

to declare, to assert to make an emphatic declaration, often public, and at times in response to pressure or an accusation

[MAT 7:23](https://ref.ly/Matt7:23) · [MAT 14:7](https://ref.ly/Matt14:7) · [JHN 1:20](https://ref.ly/Luke1:20) · [JHN 1:20](https://ref.ly/Luke1:20) · [ACT 7:17](https://ref.ly/John7:17) · [TIT 1:16](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:16) · [HEB 13:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 10:32; Luke 12:8; John 9:22; John 12:42; Acts 23:8; Romans 10:9; Romans 10:10; 1 Timothy 6:12; Titus 1:16; 1 John 2:23; 1 John 4:2; 1 John 4:3; 1 John 4:15; 2 John 1:7; Revelation 3:5; John 1:20; Acts 24:14; Hebrews 11:13; 1 John 1:9; Matthew 7:23; Matthew 14:7; Acts 7:17; Hebrews 13:15

ὁμολογία
========

confession act of expressing openly one’s allegiance to a proposition or person It is often extremely difficult, if not impossible, to translate ὁμολογέω\[a], ὁμολογία, and ἐξομολογέω\[b] by the usual expression for ‘confess,’ since this would usually imply that one has done something wrong. It is normally necessary, therefore, to employ quite a different type of relationship, usually involving a public utterance and an expression of confidence or allegiance. For example, in [MAT 10:32](https://ref.ly/Matt10:32) it may be necessary to translate ‘whoever tells people publicly that he is loyal to me, I will tell my Father that I am loyal to that person.’ Similarly, in [HEB 10:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:23) one may translate ‘let us hold on to the hope in which we have told people we have such confidence.’ Likewise, in [ROM 15:9](https://ref.ly/Acts15:9) one may translate ‘therefore I will tell the Gentiles how I have put my confidence in you.’

## ὅμως (id: 003583000000000)

ὅμως
====

ὅμως (G3676)

advb. \| ὁμοιόω

89\.74 · Relations · Concession

although, though a marker of concession, with the possible implication of an added aspect of similarity The interpretation of ὅμως in [GAL 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:15) and [1CO 14:7](https://ref.ly/Rom14:7) as indicating concession is doubtful, and it may be preferable to understand ὅμως in these contexts as meaning ‘likewise’ or ‘similarly,’ for example, ‘similarly, in the case of a human will, no one annuls it’ and ‘similarly, in the case of a flute or harp, lifeless instruments which produce a sound’ (see [64\.11](#64.11)).

[1CO 14:7](https://ref.ly/Rom14:7) · [GAL 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:15)

64\.11 · Comparison

similarly, likewise pertaining to being, at least in some respects, similar For another interpretation of ὅμως in [GAL 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:15), see [89\.74](#89.74).

[1CO 14:7](https://ref.ly/Rom14:7) · [GAL 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:15)

89\.75 · Relations · Concession

**Collocation:** ὅμως μέντοι

nevertheless, and yet a marker of an implied clause of concession1

[JHN 12:42](https://ref.ly/Luke12:42)

1 An expression meaning ‘nevertheless’ normally presupposes a concessive clause, for example, ‘although one would not have expected it, nevertheless …,’ and implies a circumstance contrary to expectation. Some languages do not have a conjunction marking a concessive clause but do have a particle or phrase for a resultant clause, for example, ‘he did not work; nevertheless he was paid,’ which is equivalent to ‘although he did not work, he was paid.’

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 14:7; Galatians 3:15; John 12:42


## ὄναρ (id: 003584000000000)

ὄναρ
====

ὄναρ (G3677)

noun, n.

33\.486 · Communication · Non\-Verbal Communication

dream a dream as a means of communication1 In a number of languages there are different terms for ‘dream’ depending upon the nature of the dream. A frightening dream without special significance or a dream with erotic implications may be referred to by terms which are quite different from a term referring to a dream which carries important meaning, in other words, a dream which constitutes a type of vision or symbol of some reality.

[MAT 1:20](https://ref.ly/Matt1:20) · [MAT 2:12](https://ref.ly/Matt2:12) · [MAT 2:13](https://ref.ly/Matt2:13) · [MAT 2:19](https://ref.ly/Matt2:19) · [MAT 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [MAT 27:19](https://ref.ly/Matt27:19)

1 The meaning of ὄναρ and ἐνύπνιον could be classified as a psychological state, but their direct connection with communication seems to justify their being included in this domain.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 1:20; Matthew 2:12; Matthew 2:13; Matthew 2:19; Matthew 2:22; Matthew 27:19

noun\-name, m. \| \-ου

93\.289 · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Onesiphorus a Christian mentioned in [2TI 1:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:16) and [2TI 4:19](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:19)

[2TI 1:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:16) · [2TI 4:19](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:19)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Timothy 1:16; 2 Timothy 4:19


## ὀνικός (id: 003591000000000)

ὀνικός
======

ὀνικός (G3684)

adjc. \| \-ή, \-όν \| ὄνος

4\.32 · Animals · Animals

of a donkey (derivative of ὄνος ‘donkey,’ [4\.31](#4.31)) pertaining to a donkey In rendering [MAT 18:6](https://ref.ly/Matt18:6) it is important to avoid the impression that a donkey would turn the millstone in the same way that a person would. Sometimes a general causative expression may be used, for example, ‘a millstone that a donkey causes to go round (rotate),’ but it may actually be necessary to say ‘... which a donkey causes to go round (rotate) by pulling on a shaft.’ In fact, a marginal note may be necessary in order to avoid serious misunderstanding and to emphasize the fact that such a millstone was large and heavy.

[MAT 18:6](https://ref.ly/Matt18:6) · [MRK 9:42](https://ref.ly/Matt9:42)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 18:6; Mark 9:42

verb

to receive help, to receive a benefit, to be favored to be the recipient of help or favor

[PHM 1:20](https://ref.ly/Titus1:20)

22:4](https://ref.ly/Jude22:4)

9\.19 · People · Human Beings

person, people (a figurative extension of meaning of ὄνομα\[a] ‘name,’ [33\.126](#33.126)) a person, with the possible implication of existence or relevance as individuals1 In some instances ἄνθρωπος\[a] ‘people’ ([9\.1](#9.1)) occurs together with ὄνομα\[b] without any significant distinction in meaning (e.g. [REV 11:13](https://ref.ly/Jude11:13)). See also [9\.20](#9.20).

[JHN 1:12](https://ref.ly/Luke1:12) · [ACT 1:15](https://ref.ly/John1:15) · [ACT 9:14](https://ref.ly/John9:14) · [ACT 10:43](https://ref.ly/John10:43) · [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2) · [1CO 1:10](https://ref.ly/Rom1:10) · [2TH 1:12](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:12) · [HEB 6:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:10) · [1JN 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:12) · [1JN 3:23](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:23) · [1JN 5:13](https://ref.ly/2Pet5:13) · [3JN 1:7](https://ref.ly/2John1:7) · [REV 3:4](https://ref.ly/Jude3:4) · [REV 11:13](https://ref.ly/Jude11:13)2

33\.265 · Communication · Witness, Testify

reputation that which is said about a person on the basis of an evaluation of the person’s conduct In obtaining a satisfactory equivalent for ‘reputation,’ it may be necessary to restructure considerably the clause in which a term such as μαρτυρία\[c] or ὄνομα\[c] occurs. For example, in [1TI 3:7](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:7) one may translate ‘he should be the kind of man that people outside the church say is a good person.’ Similarly, in [REV 3:1](https://ref.ly/Jude3:1) one may need to translate ‘people speak of you as being alive even though you are dead.’

[MRK 6:14](https://ref.ly/Matt6:14) · [JHN 2:23](https://ref.ly/Luke2:23) · [JHN 17:6](https://ref.ly/Luke17:6) · [JHN 17:26](https://ref.ly/Luke17:26) · [ROM 1:5](https://ref.ly/Acts1:5) · [3JN 1:7](https://ref.ly/2John1:7) · [REV 3:1](https://ref.ly/Jude3:1)

58\.22 · Nature, Class, Example · Class, Kind

category of, being of the type that category or kind, based upon an implied designation for a class of entities

[MAT 10:41](https://ref.ly/Matt10:41) · [MAT 10:41](https://ref.ly/Matt10:41) · [MRK 9:41](https://ref.ly/Matt9:41) · [JHN 5:43](https://ref.ly/Luke5:43) · [JHN 5:43](https://ref.ly/Luke5:43)

53\.62 · Religious Activities · Worship, Reverence

**Collocation:** ὀνομάζω τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου

to say that one belongs to the Lord or to declare that one is a worshiper of the Lord (an idiom, literally: to name the name of the Lord) to employ the name of the Lord as evidence that one worships the Lord

[2TI 2:19](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:19)

94\.20

**Collocation:** ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι

in the name of, by/with/on the authority of

33\.396 · Communication · Insult, Slander

**Collocation:** ἐκβάλλω τὸ ὄνομα

to insult, to slander (an idiom, literally: to throw out the name) to insult or slander, with a possible implication of a kind of psychological ostracism

[LUK 6:22](https://ref.ly/Mark6:22)

1 In this subdomain (Human Beings) a number of expressions referring to people are based upon metonymies involving terms for parts or aspects of persons. Such metonymic figures of speech are involved in σὰρξ καὶ αἷμα ([9\.14](#9.14)), γόνυ\[b] ([9\.16](#9.16)), χείρ\[b] ([9\.17](#9.17)), γλῶσσα\[g] ([9\.18](#9.18)), ὄνομα\[b] ([9\.19](#9.19)), and ψυχή\[c] ([9\.20](#9.20)). 2 REV 11:13 In [REV 11:13](https://ref.ly/Jude11:13) ἀνθρώπων may be regarded as a type of semantic classifier.

## ὀνομάζω (id: 003594000000000)

ὀνομάζω
=======

ὀνομάζω (G3687) — ὀνομάζομαι

verb \| ἐπονομάζομαι · εὐώνυμος · ὄνομα · τοὔνομα · ψευδώνυμος

33\.127 · Communication · Name

to call, to give a name to, to give a title to to give a name or title to

[LUK 6:14](https://ref.ly/Mark6:14) · [1CO 5:11](https://ref.ly/Rom5:11) · [EPH 1:21](https://ref.ly/Gal1:21) · [EPH 3:15](https://ref.ly/Gal3:15)

33\.133 · Communication · Name

to pronounce a name, to call out a name to utter a name in a ritual context (without directly referring to the person or speaking about the person in question)

[LUK 6:13](https://ref.ly/Mark6:13) · [ACT 19:13](https://ref.ly/John19:13)

33\.93 · Communication · Speak, Talk

to mention, to speak about to speak of something by mentioning the name of it There may be certain problems involved in translating ὀνομάζω in [EPH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Gal5:3), for the text does not mean that one should never use a term such as πλεονεξία ‘greed’ ([25\.22](#25.22)). The meaning is simply that there should never be a reason for having to speak about greed, since the people should not themselves be guilty of such behavior.

[ROM 15:20](https://ref.ly/Acts15:20) · [EPH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Gal5:3)

53\.62 · Religious Activities · Worship, Reverence

**Collocation:** ὀνομάζω τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου

to say that one belongs to the Lord or to declare that one is a worshiper of the Lord (an idiom, literally: to name the name of the Lord) to employ the name of the Lord as evidence that one worships the Lord

[2TI 2:19](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:19)

28\.22 · (ὀνομάζομαι) · Know · Known \[the content of knowledge]

to be known (a figurative extension of meaning of ὀνομάζω\[b] ‘to call a name,’ [33\.133](#33.133)) to be caused to be made known It would be possible, however, to interpret ὀνομάζομαι in [ROM 15:20](https://ref.ly/Acts15:20) in a more literal sense, so that one could translate the final part of the verse as ‘where the name of Christ had not been used’ or ‘where no one had spoken the name of Christ.’

[ROM 15:20](https://ref.ly/Acts15:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 6:14; 1 Corinthians 5:11; Ephesians 1:21; Ephesians 3:15; Luke 6:13; Acts 19:13; Romans 15:20; Ephesians 5:3; 2 Timothy 2:19


## ὄνος (id: 003595000000000)

ὄνος
====

ὄνος (G3688)

noun, m./f. \| \-ου \| ὀνάριον · ὀνικός

4\.31 · Animals · Animals

donkey, ass domestic animal belonging to the same family as the horse, but smaller and with longer ears; it can have variety of colors from dark brown, through light brown to gray and occasionally white; Equus Asinus africanus; used to ride on, carry loads, or do other work In the context of [JHN 12:15](https://ref.ly/Luke12:15) the reference is obviously to a female donkey.  
In languages in which there is no word for ‘donkey,’ it is usually best to borrow a term from a dominant language rather than attempting some type of descriptive equivalent. For example, in one language a descriptive designation, namely, ‘a long\-eared animal,’ was understood by the people as referring to a rabbit. In some languages donkey is referred to as ‘a kind of small horse’ or ‘a small horse\-like animal.’  
Though in Greek the article and/or other attributives to the noun ὄνος indicate by their forms whether ὄνος is to be understood as masculine or feminine, in a number of languages quite different terms are employed for a male or female donkey.

[MAT 21:2](https://ref.ly/Matt21:2) · [MAT 21:5](https://ref.ly/Matt21:5) · [MAT 21:7](https://ref.ly/Matt21:7) · [LUK 13:15](https://ref.ly/Mark13:15) · [JHN 12:15](https://ref.ly/Luke12:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 21:2; Matthew 21:5; Matthew 21:7; Luke 13:15; John 12:15

really, certainly, truly pertaining to actual existence1

## ὀξύς (id: 003598000000000)

ὀξύς
====

ὀξύς (G3691)

adjc. \| \-εῖα, \-ύ \| ὄξος · παροξύνομαι · παροξυσμός

79\.95 · Features of Objects · Sharp

sharp pertaining to having a thin cutting edge

[REV 1:16](https://ref.ly/Jude1:16) · [REV 2:12](https://ref.ly/Jude2:12) · [REV 14:14](https://ref.ly/Jude14:14) · [REV 14:17](https://ref.ly/Jude14:17) · [REV 14:18](https://ref.ly/Jude14:18) · [REV 14:18](https://ref.ly/Jude14:18) · [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15)

67\.112 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

quick, swift pertaining to a very short period of time, with the probable implication of special haste It would also be possible to understand ὀξεῖς οἱ πόδες αὐτῶν ‘their feet are swift’ as being an idiom, but ὀξύς occurs frequently in non\-biblical Greek with the meaning of ‘quick, swift, soon.’ In [ROM 3:15](https://ref.ly/Acts3:15) οἱ πόδες αὐτῶν is a metonymic expression meaning ‘the people,’ so that it is not simply the feet that are swift to shed blood, but it is the people who are quick to kill.

[ROM 3:15](https://ref.ly/Acts3:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 1:16; Revelation 2:12; Revelation 14:14; Revelation 14:17; Revelation 14:18; Revelation 19:15; Romans 3:15


## ὀπή (id: 003599000000000)

ὀπή
===

ὀπή (G3692)

noun, f. \| \-ῆς

1\.53 · Geographical Objects and Features · Depressions and Holes

hole, opening an opening or hole in the ground, either large as in [HEB 11:38](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:38) or relatively small as in [JAS 3:11](https://ref.ly/Heb3:11) Holes would differ from caves ([HEB 11:38](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:38)) in that holes would be vertical openings into the ground, while caves would be more or less horizontal openings into cliffs or sides of hills. In [JAS 3:11](https://ref.ly/Heb3:11) it may be quite unnecessary, and in fact confusing, to try to translate ὀπῆς, since ἐκ τῆς αὐτῆς ὀπῆς ‘from the same opening’ would seem to be redundant with ἡ πηγή ‘spring,’ since a spring would presumably only flow from a hole or opening in the ground.

[HEB 11:38](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:38) · [JAS 3:11](https://ref.ly/Heb3:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 11:38; James 3:11

prep. (with gen.), advb. \| ὀπίσω

84\.12 · Spacial Extensions · Extension From a Source

from behind, behind extension from a source which is behind

[MAT 9:20](https://ref.ly/Matt9:20) · [MAT 15:23](https://ref.ly/Matt15:23) · [MRK 5:27](https://ref.ly/Matt5:27) · [LUK 8:44](https://ref.ly/Mark8:44) · [LUK 23:26](https://ref.ly/Mark23:26)

83\.41 · Spacial Positions · In Front Of, Face To Face, In Back Of, Behind

the back of, in back of, on the back of a position on the back surface of an object

[REV 4:6](https://ref.ly/Jude4:6)

83\.21 · Spacial Positions · Around, About, Outside

on the outside, outside of the outside surface of an object The manner in which ἔξωθεν\[c], ἐκτός\[b], and ὄπισθεν\[c] may be rendered in particular contexts depends very largely upon what is being spoken about. For example, ἔξωθεν in [1PE 3:3](https://ref.ly/Jas3:3) refers to the outward aids employed in making oneself beautiful, but instead of speaking of such objects as being on the surface or on the outside, it may be necessary to speak of those things which one puts on the body or what one wears.

[REV 5:1](https://ref.ly/Jude5:1)

## ὅπλον (id: 003603000000000)

ὅπλον
=====

ὅπλον (G3696)

noun, n. \| \-ου \| ὁπλίζομαι

6\.3 · Artifacts · Artifacts \[General Meaning]

tool, instrument, means any type of tool or instrument In [ROM 6:13](https://ref.ly/Acts6:13) ὅπλον is generally interpreted as being used figuratively as a reference to parts of the body which may be instruments for doing evil.

[ROM 6:13](https://ref.ly/Acts6:13) · [ROM 6:13](https://ref.ly/Acts6:13)

6\.29 · Artifacts · Weapons and Armor

weapon an instrument used in fighting, whether offensive or defensive In the NT ὅπλον\[b] also occurs in the figurative sense of non\-material means of fighting, e.g. [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7).

[JHN 18:3](https://ref.ly/Luke18:3) · [ROM 13:12](https://ref.ly/Acts13:12) · [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7)1 · [2CO 10:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:4)

1 2CO 6:7 In [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7) ὅπλον occurs in a highly figurative context, but it is better under such circumstances to regard ὅπλον as referring to an instrument used in fighting and then to assign the figurative meaning to the entire expression. In some languages it may be necessary to translate ὅπλων in [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7) as means or simply instruments. For the understanding of the phrase τῶν δεξιῶν καὶ ἀριστερῶν as referring to offensive and defensive weapons, see [82\.7](#82.7).

89\.59 · Relations · Purpose

in order to, for the purpose of, so that a marker of purpose for events and states (sometimes occurring in highly elliptical contexts)2

[MAT 2:8](https://ref.ly/Matt2:8) · [MAT 2:23](https://ref.ly/Matt2:23) · [MAT 5:16](https://ref.ly/Matt5:16) · [MAT 5:45](https://ref.ly/Matt5:45) · [MAT 6:2](https://ref.ly/Matt6:2) · [MAT 6:4](https://ref.ly/Matt6:4) · [MAT 6:5](https://ref.ly/Matt6:5) · [MAT 6:16](https://ref.ly/Matt6:16) · [MAT 6:18](https://ref.ly/Matt6:18) · [MAT 8:17](https://ref.ly/Matt8:17) · [MAT 8:34](https://ref.ly/Matt8:34) · [MAT 9:38](https://ref.ly/Matt9:38) · [MAT 12:14](https://ref.ly/Matt12:14) · [MAT 13:35](https://ref.ly/Matt13:35) · [MAT 22:15](https://ref.ly/Matt22:15) · [MAT 23:35](https://ref.ly/Matt23:35) · [MAT 26:59](https://ref.ly/Matt26:59) · [MRK 3:6](https://ref.ly/Matt3:6) · [LUK 2:35](https://ref.ly/Mark2:35) · [LUK 7:3](https://ref.ly/Mark7:3) · [LUK 10:2](https://ref.ly/Mark10:2) · [LUK 11:37](https://ref.ly/Mark11:37) · [LUK 16:26](https://ref.ly/Mark16:26) · [LUK 16:28](https://ref.ly/Mark16:28) · [JHN 11:57](https://ref.ly/Luke11:57) · [ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20) · [ACT 8:15](https://ref.ly/John8:15) · [ACT 8:24](https://ref.ly/John8:24) · [ACT 9:2](https://ref.ly/John9:2) · [ACT 9:12](https://ref.ly/John9:12) · [ACT 9:17](https://ref.ly/John9:17) · [ACT 9:24](https://ref.ly/John9:24) · [ACT 15:17](https://ref.ly/John15:17) · [ACT 20:16](https://ref.ly/John20:16) · [ACT 23:15](https://ref.ly/John23:15) · [ACT 23:20](https://ref.ly/John23:20) · [ACT 23:23](https://ref.ly/John23:23) · [ACT 25:3](https://ref.ly/John25:3) · [ACT 25:26](https://ref.ly/John25:26) · [ROM 3:4](https://ref.ly/Acts3:4) · [ROM 9:17](https://ref.ly/Acts9:17) · [ROM 9:17](https://ref.ly/Acts9:17) · [1CO 1:29](https://ref.ly/Rom1:29) · [2CO 8:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:11) · [2CO 8:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:14) · [GAL 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:4) · [2TH 1:12](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:12) · [PHM 1:6](https://ref.ly/Titus1:6) · [HEB 2:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:9) · [HEB 9:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:15) · [JAS 5:16](https://ref.ly/Heb5:16) · [1PE 2:9](https://ref.ly/Jas2:9)

92\.17 · Discourse Referentials · Whom or What Spoken or Written About

how, somehow a reference to an indefinite means

[LUK 24:20](https://ref.ly/Mark24:20)1

1 LUK 24:20 It is also possible to interpret this clause in [LUK 24:20](https://ref.ly/Mark24:20) as representing the content of a verb of speaking. 2 ἵνα\[a] and ὅπως\[b] seem to have similar meanings and in some contexts appear to be substituted one for the other for stylistic purposes. It is, of course, possible that even in the meaning of purpose ὅπως\[b] suggests something of the manner or way in which the purpose is to be realized, but it is impossible on the basis of existing contexts to make a clear distinction between ἵνα\[a] and ὅπως\[b] when they serve as markers of purpose.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 24:20; Matthew 2:8; Matthew 2:23; Matthew 5:16; Matthew 5:45; Matthew 6:2; Matthew 6:4; Matthew 6:5; Matthew 6:16; Matthew 6:18; Matthew 8:17; Matthew 8:34; Matthew 9:38; Matthew 12:14; Matthew 13:35; Matthew 22:15; Matthew 23:35; Matthew 26:59; Mark 3:6; Luke 2:35; Luke 7:3; Luke 10:2; Luke 11:37; Luke 16:26; Luke 16:28; John 11:57; Acts 3:20; Acts 8:15; Acts 8:24; Acts 9:2; Acts 9:12; Acts 9:17; Acts 9:24; Acts 15:17; Acts 20:16; Acts 23:15; Acts 23:20; Acts 23:23; Acts 25:3; Acts 25:26; Romans 3:4; Romans 9:17; 1 Corinthians 1:29; 2 Corinthians 8:11; 2 Corinthians 8:14; Galatians 1:4; 2 Thessalonians 1:12; Philemon 1:6; Hebrews 2:9; Hebrews 9:15; James 5:16; 1 Peter 2:9


## ὅραμα (id: 003612000000000)

ὅραμα
=====

ὅραμα (G3705)

noun, n. \| \-τος \| ὁράω

24\.2 · Sensory Events and States · See

something seen, sight (derivative of ὁράω\[a] ‘to see,’ [24\.1](#24.1)) that which is seen1

[ACT 7:31](https://ref.ly/John7:31)2

33\.488 · Communication · Non\-Verbal Communication

vision an event in which something appears vividly and credibly to the mind, although not actually present, but implying the influence of some divine or supernatural power or agency

[MAT 17:9](https://ref.ly/Matt17:9) · [ACT 9:10](https://ref.ly/John9:10) · [ACT 9:12](https://ref.ly/John9:12) · [ACT 10:3](https://ref.ly/John10:3) · [ACT 10:17](https://ref.ly/John10:17) · [ACT 10:19](https://ref.ly/John10:19) · [ACT 11:5](https://ref.ly/John11:5) · [ACT 12:9](https://ref.ly/John12:9) · [ACT 16:9](https://ref.ly/John16:9) · [ACT 16:10](https://ref.ly/John16:10) · [ACT 18:9](https://ref.ly/John18:9)

1 ὅραμα may also occur in the meaning of ‘vision,’ a type of non\-verbal communication in [33\.488](#33.488), Communication. 2 ACT 7:31 It may seem strange to have in the same expression ἰδών and τὸ ὅραμα, for these would seem to be redundant. However, this expression in [ACT 7:31](https://ref.ly/John7:31) may be rendered as ‘as Moses looked, he was amazed by what he saw.’

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 7:31; Matthew 17:9; Acts 9:10; Acts 9:12; Acts 10:3; Acts 10:17; Acts 10:19; Acts 11:5; Acts 12:9; Acts 16:9; Acts 16:10; Acts 18:9


## ὅρασις (id: 003613000000000)

ὅρασις
======

ὅρασις (G3706)

noun, f. \| \-εως \| ὁράω

33\.488 · Communication · Non\-Verbal Communication

vision an event in which something appears vividly and credibly to the mind, although not actually present, but implying the influence of some divine or supernatural power or agency

[ACT 2:17](https://ref.ly/John2:17) · [REV 9:17](https://ref.ly/Jude9:17)

24\.31 · Sensory Events and States · See

appearance that which appears or is seen ὅρασις\[b] in [REV 4:3](https://ref.ly/Jude4:3) may be rendered in some languages as ‘as seen’ or ‘to the eyes’ or ‘to look at,’ for example, ‘and he who sat upon it was similar to a jasper stone to look at’ or ‘to look at the one who sat on it was like looking at a jasper stone.’

[REV 4:3](https://ref.ly/Jude4:3) · [REV 4:3](https://ref.ly/Jude4:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 2:17; Revelation 9:17; Revelation 4:3


## ὁρατός (id: 003614000000000)

ὁρατός
======

ὁρατός (G3707)

adjc. \| \-ή, \-όν \| ὁράω

24\.3 · Sensory Events and States · See

what can be seen, visible pertaining to that which can be seen In a number of languages it may be wise to render ‘visible and invisible’ as an active expression with ‘people’ as the subject, for example, ‘all things were created by him ... both those things which people can see and those things which people cannot see’ or ‘... what people can see and what they cannot see.’

[COL 1:16](https://ref.ly/Phil1:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Colossians 1:16


## ὁράω (id: 003615000000000)

ὁράω
====

ὁράω (G3708) — εἶδον, ἴδε, ἰδού

verb \| impf.3pl. ἑώρων · fut. ὄψομαι · aor. εἶδα, εἶδον · ptc. ἰδών · impv. ἴδετε · pf. ἑώρακα, ἑόρακα · aor.pass. ὤφθην · ptc. ὀφθείς · fut.pass. ὀφθήσομαι \| ἀόρατος · αὐτόπτης · ἀφοράω · ἐνώπιον · ἐποπτεύω · ἐπόπτης · ἔσοπτρον · εὐπροσωπέω · ἐφοράω · καθοράω · κατενώπιον · μυωπάζω · ὀπτάνομαι · ὀπτασία · ὅραμα · ὅρασις · ὁρατός · ὀφθαλμοδουλία · ὄψις · προοράω · προσωπολημπτέω · προσωπολήμπτης · προσωπολημψία · πρόσωπον · σκυθρωπός · συνοράω · ὑπεροράω · ὑπωπιάζω · φρουρέω

30\.45 · Think · To Think About, with the Implied Purpose of Responding Appropriately

to take notice of, to consider, to pay attention to, to concern oneself with to take special notice of something, with the implication of concerning oneself Though the terms ἐπιβλέπω, ἐπεῖδον (ἐφοράω), and ὁράω suggest as a result of their central meanings some measure of visual perception, what is involved in these specific contexts is much more than visual perception. The focus is actually upon intellectual activity and concern.

32\.11 · Understand · Come To Understand

to understand, to perceive, to see, to recognize to come to understand as the result of perception

[JHN 8:38](https://ref.ly/Luke8:38) · [ACT 8:23](https://ref.ly/John8:23) · [HEB 12:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:14) · [JAS 2:24](https://ref.ly/Heb2:24)

34\.50 · Association · Visit

to visit, to go to see to go to see a person on the basis of friendship and with helpful intent

90\.79 · Case · Experiencer

to experience, to undergo (a figurative extension of meaning of ὁράω\[a] ‘to see,’ [24\.1](#24.1)) to experience an event or state, normally in negative expressions indicating what one will not experience

91\.10 · (ἰδού\[b]) · Discourse Markers · Markers of Emphasis
